data.heading: Publications
data.focus: A curated collection of studies on productivity, thermodynamics of waiting, and the perfect playlist—because science should also be enjoyable.

featured.alt: Coffee and notes on a desk
featured.title: How to Look Like You're Working: Techniques for Appearing Productive
paragraphs: In the demanding environment of graduate school, maintaining the appearance of constant intellectual progress is essential, and your coffee mug is your most versatile tool. By keeping a cluttered desk piled high with dense journal articles, strategically weighted down by a half-empty mug to create authentic coffee rings, you signal to passing advisors that you are deep in a literature review. When anyone approaches, simply stare intensely at a complex chart, take a slow sip of the lukewarm brew, and mutter something vague about “methodological limitations.” This simple setup reframes your morning caffeine routine as a rigorous, uninterrupted research session.

Finally, during department seminars and group meetings, the physical act of drinking coffee serves as an excellent defense mechanism against unexpected questions. If a professor asks you to defend a weak point in your hypothesis, do not panic; instead, slowly raise your cup, take a measured sip, and hold the pose for several seconds. This silence is perceived not as a lack of preparation, but as the quiet contemplation of a serious academic formulating a nuanced response. Whether your mug is filled with actual coffee or merely hot water, using it as a prop allows you to navigate difficult academic inquiries with an air of quiet confidence.

items.title: The Kettle Takes Too Long: A Thermodynamic Lament
items.summary: An empirical investigation into the subjective perception of time during water heating, and its correlation with morning caffeine craving intensity.

items.title: Finding a Good Playlist: Algorithmic Approaches to Mood Matching
items.summary: A review of recommendation algorithms for matching musical ambiance to coffee brewing methods and time of day, revealing a strong tempo-caffeine correlation. items.title: Neural Synchronization in Caffeine-Rich Environments
items.summary: This study examines collective neuro-signatures of coffee drinkers under deadline pressure, revealing how synchronized caffeine intake correlates with breakthrough ideas in open-plan offices.
